elements.sent-2411-001: For $g\in\BMOA$, we introduce the meromorphic optimal domain $(T_g,H^p)$, i.e. the space containing the meromorphic functions that are mapped under the action of the generalized Volterra operator $T_g$ into the Hardy space $H^p$.
elements.sent-2411-002: We investigate its properties and characterize for which $g_1,g_2 \in \BMOA$ the corresponding meromorphic optimal domains coincide.
elements.sent-2411-003: This investigation contributes to a more comprehensive understanding of the holomorphic optimal domain of $T_g$ in $H^p$.
elements.sent-2411-004: Let $\m{D}$ be the unit disc of the complex plane, $\m{T}$ be its boundary, and $\Hol(\m{D )$ the space of analytic functions defined in $\m{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-005: The (shifted) Ces\'aro summation operator is defined as \begin{equation}C(f)(z)=\int_{0}^{z}\frac{f(\zeta)}{1-\zeta}\,d\zeta\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-006: For a fixed $g\in \Hol(\m{D )$, we consider the generalized Volterra operator, acting on the space of holomorphic functions, as \begin{equation}\label{equation for Tg} T_g(f)(z)=\int_{0}^{z}f(\zeta)g'(\zeta)\,d\zeta\,,\qquad z\in\m{D},\quad f\in\Hol(\m{D})\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-007: The motivation for studying this operator arises from the fact that, as $g$ varies within $\Hol(\m{D )$, $T_g$ represents significant classical operators.}
elements.sent-2411-008: For instance, if $g(z)=z$, then $T_g$ is the classical Volterra operator, while, choosing $g(z)=-\log(1-z)$, then $T_g$ coincides with the Ces\'aro summation operator.
elements.sent-2411-009: The primary focus in studying $T_g$ operators is their actions on spaces of analytic functions, with particular emphasis on the Hardy spaces of the unit disc.
elements.sent-2411-010: Let $0<p<\infty$, the Hardy space $H^p$ consists of all functions $f\in \Hol(\mathbb{D )$ such that \begin{equation}\|f\|^p_{H^p}=\sup_{0\leq r<1}\int_{0}^{2\pi}|f(re^{it})|^p\,\frac{dt}{2\pi}<\infty\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-011: For $p=\infty$, $H^{\infty}$ denotes the space of bounded analytic functions in the unit disc, equipped with the supremum norm.. \par Ch.
elements.sent-2411-012: Pommerenke \cite{pommerenke1977schlichte proved that the space of symbols $g$ for which $T_g$ is bounded on the Hilbert space $H^2$ is $\BMOA$.. In continuation, A.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Siskakis \cite{aleman1995integral showed that the space of symbols $g$ for which $T_g$ is bounded on the Hardy space $H^p$, for $1\leq p<\infty$, is still $\BMOA$.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The space of analytic functions of bounded mean oscillation, $\BMOA$, consists of all $f \in H^2$ such that \begin{equation}\|f\|_*:=\sup_{a\in D} \| f \circ \varphi_a - f(a) \|_{H^2} < \infty,  where  \varphi_a(z) = \frac{z-a}{1-\overline{a}z}\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-016: Subsequently, the study of the boundedness and the properties \CB of $T_g$ operators on various spaces of analytic functions attracted a lot of attention, see, for example, \cite{aleman1997integration , \cite{wu2006areabergman , \cite{pelaez2014weightedmemoir , \cite{TanausuGalanopoulostentspaces , \cite{Chalmoukis2024 and \cite{bellavita2024 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: For fixed $g\in\BMOA$, even though $T_g(H^p)\subset H^p$, there exists function $f$ not belonging to $H^p$, for which $T_g(f)$ is still in $H^p$, see \cite[Theorem 1]{Bellavitaoptimaldom .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In \cite{Curbera2011 and \cite{curbera2012 , G.. Curbera and W.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Ricker introduced the optimal domain for the Ces\'aro operator $[C,H^p]$, consisting of all the analytic functions which are mapped by $C$ into $H^p$.
elements.sent-2411-020: In \cite{Bellavitaoptimaldom , the authors defined and studied the optimal domain for integral operator $T_g$ \begin{equation}[T_g,H^p]=\{f\in \Hol(\m{D})\colon T_g(f) \in H^p\}\, ,\end{equation} It consists of all the analytic functions mapped by $T_g$ into $H^p$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In \cite{Bellavitaoptimaldom , the authors observed that such property does not hold for $[T_g,H^p]$ for a general symbol $g\in\BMOA$, since $g'$ may have zeros inside the unit disc.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Hence, it is clear that we need to relax the condition $f\in \Hol(\m{D )$.}
elements.sent-2411-023: We denote as $\Mer(\m{D )$ the set of meromorphic functions defined in the unit disc $\m{D}$.}
